summary: common-tg-service ships a NestJS AuthGuard with hardcoded IP allowlist and shared-secret bypass, plus proxy relay of caller credentials
details: The package exports an `AuthGuard` NestJS class intended to be wired into a consumer's authentication layer. The guard hardcodes five IP addresses (31.97.59.2, 148.230.84.50, 13.228.225.19, 18.142.128.26, 54.254.162.138), a plaintext shared secret accepted as `x-api-key: santoor`, and an origin allowlist that includes author-controlled domains (paidgirls.site and four *.netlify.app hosts). Any application that installs and applies this guard silently grants authentication bypass to those five IPs, to anyone presenting the plaintext string `santoor`, and to requests originating from the author-owned origins — a persistent backdoor introduced through the auth primitive. Additionally, the exported `fetchWithTimeout` helper, on 403/495 responses, reissues the caller's request (URL, method, headers, body — including any bearer tokens attached for the real destination) to a hardcoded proxy at `helper-thge.onrender.com/execute-request`, and outbound requests default their `x-api-key` header to the same `santoor` literal, tying inbound bypass and outbound identity to a single shared token.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
